body.p:
XQueryProgramming LanguagesXQuery leads a shadowy existence in the publishing field. During my time as an XSL programmer for two publishing companies, I never had anything to do with it. Only when I was closer to the actual data and worked with XML databases did I come into contact with XQuery.
UWE test footnote (38/40, callout L): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
While relational databases are queried with SQL, XML databases such as eXist eXist or MarkLogic MarkLogic use XQuery as their query language.
Individual XML documents can also be queried, for example, in Oxygen XML EditorToolsoXygen XML Editor using the XQuery Builder tool XQuery Builder (oXygen) or via the Saxon command line:
The -qs option allows you to specify the query string.

As the simple example already shows, XQuery is related to XPath. XQuery encompasses the XPath language scope but additionally offers the FLOWR syntax for more powerful queries. Using further extensions XQuery extensions, entire programs can be created that go far beyond the functionality of a "query language".

section.title: XQuery Builder
section.p: Oxygen XML Editor oXygen XML Editor offers a convenient way to execute XQuery queries on a loaded XML document. You can write your query in the corresponding input window.

fig.title: oXygen XQuery Builder
fig.desc: The oXygen XQuery Builder allows you to easily test queries

section.title: FLOWR Expression
section.p: FLOWRFLOWR Expression stands for for, let, where, order by, return. These are the query instructions allowed in the expression — in exactly this order.
section.pre: let $bibliothek := . for $x in $bibliothek//buecher, $y in $bibliothek//autoren/autor where starts-with($autor, 'Grass') and $x/@autorId = $y/@id return $x/titel
section.p: This query returns the titles of all books by Grass. Notable here is the syntax.

note.p: Normally, you would expect a block terminator such as a semicolon between individual statements. But since we are programming functionally here, things are a bit different...

section.title: XML via XQuery
section.p: It is also possible to generate XML, although for transformations XSLT is of course preferable. Tags are written directly into the expression, as shown here:
If you save this snippet in a file buecher.xquery, you can search a buecher.xml input file using the following command line:

section.title: Document Projection
section.p: Document Projection Verstecktes Saxon Feature: Dokument Projektion bei einer XQuery Abfrage Tips and TricksDocument Projection is a hidden Saxon XQuery feature. It works only for a single query. This can be quite helpful when you want to search a document that is several 100 MB in size.
Without projection, the above example would be processed as follows:
section.pre: java -cp usr/lib/saxon/saxon.jar net.sf.saxon.Query -t -s:buecher.xml -q:buecher.xquery -o:ergebnis.xml -projection:off nachname=grass Saxon-EE 9.7.0.20J from Saxonica Java version 1.8.0_60 Using license serial number V005095 Analyzing query from Desktop/buecher.xquery Generating byte code... Analysis time: 201.10095 milliseconds Processing file:/Users/Alex/buecher.xml Using parser com.sun.org.apache.xerces.internal.jaxp.SAXParserImpl$JAXPSAXParser Building tree for file:/Users/Alex/buecher.xml using class net.sf.saxon.tree.tiny.TinyBuilder Tree built in 3.482278ms Tree size: 46 nodes, 58 characters, 6 attributes Execution time: 27.137589ms Memory used: 67031664
section.p: With the -projection:on option, the execution time changes significantly:
section.pre:
[...]
Document projection for file:/Users/Alex/buecher.xml -- Input nodes 50; output nodes 27; reduction = 46% Tree built in 3.80615ms Tree size: 26 nodes, 58 characters, 3 attributes Execution time: 15.83463ms Memory used: 64339064
